Logistic BHM - RStan v JAGS v Nimble - 1

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • View Source

Quickstart

This vignette compares the same Bayesian hierarchical model fitted first with Rstan and then with Vegas. The model is a logistic regression. Only key snippets and outputs are shown below. The full vignette is available here. For the equivalent vignette but which uses Apple’s MLX framework to run the computation’s on a GPU see here. If available the latter approach is preferred as considerably computationally faster.

Model Formulation

The model implemented here is a non-centered parameterization (helps improve MCMC sampling) for a logistic model.

\[ \begin{aligned} \mu_0 &\sim \text{Normal}(0, 2.5)\\ \sigma_0 &\sim \text{Half-Normal}(0, 2.5) \\ \mu_1 &\sim \text{Normal}(0, 2.5) \\ \sigma_1 &\sim \text{Half-Normal}(0, 2.5) \\ \tilde{\theta}_j &\sim \text{Normal}(0, 1)\quad\enspace\text{for}\enspace{j=1,2} \\ \beta_0 &= \mu_0 + \sigma_0 \tilde{\theta}_1 \\ \beta_1 &= \mu_1 + \sigma_1 \tilde{\theta}_2 \\ \text{logit(}{p_i}) &= \beta_0 + \beta_1 z_i\quad\quad\enspace\enspace\text{for}\enspace{i=1,\dots,N} \\ y_i &\sim \text{Bernoulli}(p_i) \end{aligned} \]

Data set

The R chunk below creates a simple dataset of three cols:

  • a binary response variable y (0/1 = non-responder/responder),
  • a (dummy) basket ID variable (=1)
  • a binary treatment variable (0/1 = control/test treatment)

The basket ID is currently set fixed at 1, denoting there is only one basket in this trial, i.e. a classical two arm randomized trial design. Baskets will be added in other vignettes.

Code 
### Prepare model inputs
set.seed(99999)
# Set up data
rr_k_ctrl <- c(0.20)        # control response rate for each basket
rr_k_trt <- c(0.40)         # treatment response rate for each basket

K<-length(rr_k_ctrl)        # number of baskets

N_k_ctrl <- rep(100, K)     # number of control participants per basket
N_k_trt <- rep(100, K)      # number of treatment participants per basket
N_k <- N_k_ctrl + N_k_trt   # number of participants per basket (both arms combined)
N <- sum(N_k)               # total sample size
k_vec <- rep(1:K, N_k)      # N x 1 vector of basket indicators (1 to K)

z_vec<-NULL;
y<-NULL;
for(i in 1:K){ # for each basket repeat 0-control 1-trt according to the specifc Ns
  z_vec<-c(z_vec,rep(0:1,c(N_k_ctrl[i],N_k_trt[i]))) # treatment/control indicator
  y<-c(y,
       c(rbinom(N_k_ctrl[i],1,rr_k_ctrl[i]), # bernoulli for control
         rbinom(N_k_trt[i],1,rr_k_trt[i]))) #           for trt
}

thedata<-data.frame(y,basketID=k_vec,Treatment=z_vec)
Code 
my_table1_object <- head(thedata) %>%
  kable() %>%
  kable_styling(full_width = F) %>%
  column_spec(1, bold = F)#, color = "blue")
#> Warning: 'xfun::attr()' is deprecated.
#> Use 'xfun::attr2()' instead.
#> See help("Deprecated")

#> Warning: 'xfun::attr()' is deprecated.
#> Use 'xfun::attr2()' instead.
#> See help("Deprecated")

my_table1_object
y basketID Treatment
0 1 0
1 1 0
0 1 0
1 1 0
0 1 0
0 1 0

RStan

The code for implementing the above model is RStan is given below.

Code 
### Stan model
  BHM_stan_1 <- "data {
  int<lower = 0> K;                     // number of baskets (K >= 2)
  int<lower = 0> N;                     // total number of participants
  //int<lower = 0, upper = N> N_k[K];     // K x 1 vector of basket sample sizes
  int<lower = 0, upper = N> N_k;
  int<lower = 1, upper = K> k_vec[N];   // N x 1 vector of basket indicators
  int<lower = 0, upper = 1> z_vec[N];   // N x 1 vector of treatment indicators for active treatment arm
  int<lower = 0, upper = 1> y[N];       // N x 1 vector of binary responses
}
parameters {
  matrix[K,2] beta_tr;                  // K x 2 matrix of transformed regression coefficients
  real mu1;                              // scalar of hierarchical mean (log odds ratio)
  real<lower = 0> sigma1;                  // scalar of hierarchical SD (log odds ratio)
  real mu0;                              // scalar of hierarchical mean (log odds ratio)
  real<lower = 0> sigma0;                  // scalar of hierarchical SD (log odds ratio)

}
transformed parameters {
  matrix[K,2] beta;                     // K x 2 matrix of regression coefficients (without transformation)
  // Calculate beta coefs using transformed betas (leads to better mixing)
  for (k in 1:K){
    beta[k,1] = mu0 + sigma0 * beta_tr[k,1];
    beta[k,2] = mu1 + sigma1 * beta_tr[k,2];
  }

}
model {
  mu0 ~ normal(0, 2.5);    // vectorized normal priors for hierarchical means (specify SD in normal distn)
  sigma0 ~ normal(0, 2.5);       // vectorized half-normal priors for hierarchical SDs (specify SD in normal distn)
  mu1 ~ normal(0, 2.5);    // vectorized normal priors for hierarchical means (specify SD in normal distn)
  sigma1 ~ normal(0, 2.5);       // vectorized half-normal priors for hierarchical SDs (specify SD in normal distn)
  beta_tr[,1] ~ normal(0, 1);  // vectorized normal prior for the log odds for the control arm
  beta_tr[,2] ~ normal(0, 1);  // vectorized normal prior for the log odds ratios
  for (i in 1:N)
    y[i] ~ bernoulli_logit(beta[k_vec[i], 1] + beta[k_vec[i], 2] * z_vec[i]);
}
"

Diagnostics

Code 
# Log posterior
lp_fit_2 <- log_posterior(stan_fit_2)
head(lp_fit_2)
#>   Chain Iteration     Value
#> 1     1         1 -126.7391
#> 2     1         2 -126.9268
#> 3     1         3 -126.6191
#> 4     1         4 -130.3378
#> 5     1         5 -129.2852
#> 6     1         6 -129.0437

#Nuts patterns
np_fit_2 <- nuts_params(stan_fit_2)
head(np_fit_2)
#>   Chain Iteration     Parameter     Value
#> 1     1         1 accept_stat__ 0.7296036
#> 2     1         2 accept_stat__ 0.9304792
#> 3     1         3 accept_stat__ 0.8886512
#> 4     1         4 accept_stat__ 0.8099917
#> 5     1         5 accept_stat__ 0.9389580
#> 6     1         6 accept_stat__ 0.9893142

#mcmc parcoord
color_scheme_set("darkgray")
posterior_fit_2 <- as.array(stan_fit_2)
mcmc_parcoord(posterior_fit_2, np = np_fit_2)

Summarize results

Code 
library(bayesplot)
color_scheme_set("viridisA")
p<-mcmc_trace(stan_fit_2,"mu0")
#ggsave("precomputed/vg2_traceplot1_stan.png", p)
print(p)
Code 

plot(stan_fit_2)
#> ci_level: 0.8 (80% intervals)
#> outer_level: 0.95 (95% intervals)

JAGS

Code 
BHM_jags_2 <- "
model {
for (i in 1:N) {
    p[i] <- ilogit(beta[k_vec[i], 1] + beta[k_vec[i], 2] * z_vec[i])
    y[i] ~ dbern(p[i])
  }

  for (k in 1:K) {
    beta_tr[k, 1] ~ dnorm(0, 1)
    beta_tr[k, 2] ~ dnorm(0, 1)
  }
  
  for (j in 1:K){
    beta[j,1] <- mu0 + sigma0 * beta_tr[j,1]
    beta[j,2] <- mu1 + sigma1 * beta_tr[j,2]
  }

  #tau0 <- 1 / (sigma0^2)
  #tau1 <- 1 / (sigma1^2)

  tau_mu <- 1 / (2.5^2)

  mu0 ~ dnorm(0, tau_mu)
  mu1 ~ dnorm(0, tau_mu)

  sigma0 ~ dunif(0, 3)
  sigma1 ~ dunif(0, 3)
}
"

bhm_jags_mod <- jags.model(
  textConnection(BHM_jags_2),
  data = data_input_1,
  n.chains = 4,
  n.adapt = 10000
)
#> Warning in jags.model(textConnection(BHM_jags_2), data = data_input_1, n.chains
#> = 4, : Unused variable "N_k" in data
#> Compiling model graph
#>    Resolving undeclared variables
#>    Allocating nodes
#> Graph information:
#>    Observed stochastic nodes: 200
#>    Unobserved stochastic nodes: 6
#>    Total graph size: 625
#> 
#> Initializing model

update(bhm_jags_mod, 10000)

jags_mod_sim = coda.samples(model = bhm_jags_mod,
                       variable.names = c("mu0", "sigma0","mu1", "sigma1", "beta","beta_tr"),
                       n.iter = 10000, thin =1)

summary(jags_mod_sim)
#> 
#> Iterations = 20001:30000
#> Thinning interval = 1 
#> Number of chains = 4 
#> Sample size per chain = 10000 
#> 
#> 1. Empirical mean and standard deviation for each variable,
#>    plus standard error of the mean:
#> 
#>                 Mean     SD Naive SE Time-series SE
#> beta[1,1]    -1.1532 0.2362 0.001181       0.002049
#> beta[1,2]     0.9462 0.3102 0.001551       0.002780
#> beta_tr[1,1] -0.1493 0.9010 0.004505       0.044807
#> beta_tr[1,2]  0.1184 0.8814 0.004407       0.034779
#> mu0          -0.8550 1.2741 0.006371       0.088521
#> mu1           0.7410 1.2891 0.006446       0.071929
#> sigma0        1.3755 0.8430 0.004215       0.021647
#> sigma1        1.3849 0.8525 0.004263       0.018292
#> 
#> 2. Quantiles for each variable:
#> 
#>                  2.5%     25%     50%     75%   97.5%
#> beta[1,1]    -1.63106 -1.3094 -1.1495 -0.9898 -0.7052
#> beta[1,2]     0.34580  0.7343  0.9473  1.1523  1.5570
#> beta_tr[1,1] -1.88508 -0.7560 -0.1661  0.4353  1.6485
#> beta_tr[1,2] -1.63303 -0.4448  0.1137  0.6938  1.8171
#> mu0          -3.36580 -1.5633 -0.9919 -0.1898  2.0870
#> mu1          -2.19268  0.1178  0.8368  1.4256  3.3212
#> sigma0        0.06532  0.6566  1.3082  2.0685  2.9026
#> sigma1        0.06731  0.6390  1.3340  2.0986  2.8989

#plot(jags_mod_sim)

mat_jags <- as.matrix(jags_mod_sim)
beta_jags <- mat_jags[, "beta[1,1]"]

NIMBLE with default sampler

Code 
nimble_code_1 <- nimbleCode({  

  for (i in 1:N) {
    p[i] <- ilogit(beta[k_vec[i], 1] + beta[k_vec[i], 2] * z_vec[i])
    y[i] ~ dbern(p[i])
  }

  for (k in 1:K) {
    beta_tr[k, 1] ~ dnorm(0, 1)
    beta_tr[k, 2] ~ dnorm(0, 1)
  }
  
  for (j in 1:K){
    beta[j,1] <- mu0 + sigma0 * beta_tr[j,1]
    beta[j,2] <- mu1 + sigma1 * beta_tr[j,2]
  }

  #tau0 <- 1 / (sigma0^2)
  #tau1 <- 1 / (sigma1^2)

  tau_mu <- 1 / (2.5^2)

  mu0 ~ dnorm(0, tau_mu)
  mu1 ~ dnorm(0, tau_mu)

  sigma0 ~ dunif(0, 3)
  sigma1 ~ dunif(0, 3)
  }

) 

#Initial values

K_val <- data_input_1$K
N_val <- data_input_1$N

inits <- list(
  mu0 = 0,
  mu1 = 0,
  sigma0 = 1,
  sigma1 = 1,
  beta_tr = matrix(0, nrow = K_val, ncol = 2),
  beta = matrix(0, nrow = K_val, ncol = 2)
)

# Build model


model <- nimbleModel(
  nimble_code_1,
  # Ensure k_vec is in constants, not data
  constants = list(
    N = N_val,
    K = K_val,
    k_vec = data_input_1$k_vec,
    z_vec = data_input_1$z_vec
  ),
  data = list(
    y = data_input_1$y
  ),
  inits = inits,
  dimensions = list(
    beta_tr = c(K_val, 2),
    beta = c(K_val, 2),
    k_vec = c(N_val)    
  )
)
#> Defining model
#> Building model
#> Setting data and initial values
#> Running calculate on model
#>   [Note] Any error reports that follow may simply reflect missing values in model variables.
#> Checking model sizes and dimensions

#Configure the default sampler
# NIMBLE automatically detects conjugacy (Gibbs) and assigns 
# Adaptive Metropolis to non-conjugate parameters.
mcmcConf <- configureMCMC(model)
#> ===== Monitors =====
#> thin = 1: beta_tr, mu0, mu1, sigma0, sigma1
#> ===== Samplers =====
#> RW sampler (6)
#>   - beta_tr[]  (2 elements)
#>   - sigma0
#>   - sigma1
#>   - mu0
#>   - mu1
mcmcConf$addMonitors("beta")
#> thin = 1: beta, beta_tr, mu0, mu1, sigma0, sigma1
#conf$addMonitors(c("mu0", "mu1", "sigma0", "sigma1", "beta_tr", "beta"))



#Build MCMC
mcmc <- buildMCMC(mcmcConf)

# Compile
Cmodel <- compileNimble(model)
#> Compiling
#>   [Note] This may take a minute.
#>   [Note] Use 'showCompilerOutput = TRUE' to see C++ compilation details.

# Build and compile HMC sampler
Cmcmc <- compileNimble(mcmc, project = model)
#> Compiling
#>   [Note] This may take a minute.
#>   [Note] Use 'showCompilerOutput = TRUE' to see C++ compilation details.

# Run the sampler

samples <- runMCMC(
  Cmcmc,
  niter = 20000,
  nburnin = 5000,
  thin = 10,
  nchains = 4,
  samplesAsCodaMCMC = TRUE
)
#> running chain 1...
#> |-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
#> |-------------------------------------------------------|
#> running chain 2...
#> |-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
#> |-------------------------------------------------------|
#> running chain 3...
#> |-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
#> |-------------------------------------------------------|
#> running chain 4...
#> |-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
#> |-------------------------------------------------------|

# Diagnosis

library(coda)

#plot(samples)
summary(samples)
#> 
#> Iterations = 1:1500
#> Thinning interval = 1 
#> Number of chains = 4 
#> Sample size per chain = 1500 
#> 
#> 1. Empirical mean and standard deviation for each variable,
#>    plus standard error of the mean:
#> 
#>                   Mean     SD Naive SE Time-series SE
#> beta[1, 1]    -1.15029 0.2364 0.003052       0.003410
#> beta[1, 2]     0.94509 0.3094 0.003994       0.004429
#> beta_tr[1, 1] -0.19536 0.8277 0.010686       0.050781
#> beta_tr[1, 2]  0.02848 0.8729 0.011269       0.051470
#> mu0           -0.76893 1.1903 0.015367       0.105124
#> mu1            0.91438 1.2973 0.016748       0.098578
#> sigma0         1.34591 0.8660 0.011180       0.035687
#> sigma1         1.38666 0.8515 0.010993       0.025678
#> 
#> 2. Quantiles for each variable:
#> 
#>                   2.5%     25%      50%     75%   97.5%
#> beta[1, 1]    -1.62777 -1.3072 -1.14750 -0.9869 -0.7022
#> beta[1, 2]     0.34085  0.7363  0.94118  1.1489  1.5611
#> beta_tr[1, 1] -1.88029 -0.7193 -0.19443  0.3043  1.5031
#> beta_tr[1, 2] -1.82544 -0.5379  0.06995  0.6059  1.7073
#> mu0           -2.85484 -1.4356 -0.95031 -0.2643  2.3365
#> mu1           -1.72061  0.2313  0.87417  1.5192  3.9361
#> sigma0         0.05751  0.5821  1.27207  2.0821  2.9032
#> sigma1         0.06569  0.6589  1.32679  2.0909  2.9011
gelman.diag(samples)
#> Potential scale reduction factors:
#> 
#>               Point est. Upper C.I.
#> beta[1, 1]          1.00       1.00
#> beta[1, 2]          1.00       1.00
#> beta_tr[1, 1]       1.03       1.08
#> beta_tr[1, 2]       1.08       1.23
#> mu0                 1.08       1.20
#> mu1                 1.11       1.29
#> sigma0              1.01       1.02
#> sigma1              1.01       1.02
#> 
#> Multivariate psrf
#> 
#> 1.12
effectiveSize(samples)
#>    beta[1, 1]    beta[1, 2] beta_tr[1, 1] beta_tr[1, 2]           mu0 
#>     4886.7952     4896.1995      307.8632      307.2655      170.3996 
#>           mu1        sigma0        sigma1 
#>      187.2001      769.1366     1112.3203

all_samples <- do.call(rbind, samples)

beta_nimble1 <- all_samples[, "beta[1, 1]"]

NIMBLE with HMC

Code 
nimble_code_1 <- nimbleCode({  

  for (i in 1:N) {
    p[i] <- ilogit(beta[k_vec[i], 1] + beta[k_vec[i], 2] * z_vec[i])
    y[i] ~ dbern(p[i])
  }

  for (k in 1:K) {
    beta_tr[k, 1] ~ dnorm(0, 1)
    beta_tr[k, 2] ~ dnorm(0, 1)
  }
  
  for (j in 1:K){
    beta[j,1] <- mu0 + sigma0 * beta_tr[j,1]
    beta[j,2] <- mu1 + sigma1 * beta_tr[j,2]
  }

  #tau0 <- 1 / (sigma0^2)
  #tau1 <- 1 / (sigma1^2)

  tau_mu <- 1 / (2.5^2)

  mu0 ~ dnorm(0, tau_mu)
  mu1 ~ dnorm(0, tau_mu)

  sigma0 ~ dunif(0, 3)
  sigma1 ~ dunif(0, 3)
  }

) 

#Initial values

K_val <- data_input_1$K
N_val <- data_input_1$N

inits <- list(
  mu0 = 0,
  mu1 = 0,
  sigma0 = 1,
  sigma1 = 1,
  beta_tr = matrix(0, nrow = K_val, ncol = 2),
  beta = matrix(0, nrow = K_val, ncol = 2)
)

# Build model


model <- nimbleModel(
  nimble_code_1,
  # Ensure k_vec is in constants, not data
  constants = list(
    N = N_val,
    K = K_val,
    k_vec = data_input_1$k_vec,
    z_vec = data_input_1$z_vec
  ),
  data = list(
    y = data_input_1$y
  ),
  inits = inits,
  dimensions = list(
    beta_tr = c(K_val, 2),
    beta = c(K_val, 2),
    k_vec = c(N_val)    # This is often the missing piece!
  ),
  buildDerivs = TRUE
)
#> Defining model
#> Building model
#> Setting data and initial values
#> Running calculate on model
#>   [Note] Any error reports that follow may simply reflect missing values in model variables.
#> Checking model sizes and dimensions

# Compile
Cmodel <- compileNimble(model)
#> Compiling
#>   [Note] This may take a minute.
#>   [Note] Use 'showCompilerOutput = TRUE' to see C++ compilation details.

# Key step: Configure HMC

conf <- configureHMC(model)
#> ===== Monitors =====
#> thin = 1: beta_tr, mu0, mu1, sigma0, sigma1
#> ===== Samplers =====
#> NUTS sampler (1)
#>   - beta_tr[1, 1], beta_tr[1, 2], sigma0, sigma1, mu0, mu1
conf$addMonitors(c("mu0", "mu1", "sigma0", "sigma1", "beta_tr", "beta"))
#> thin = 1: beta, beta_tr, mu0, mu1, sigma0, sigma1
conf$printSamplers()
#> [1] NUTS sampler: beta_tr[1, 1], beta_tr[1, 2], sigma0, sigma1, mu0, mu1

# Build and compile HMC sampler
Rmcmc <- buildMCMC(conf)
Cmcmc <- compileNimble(Rmcmc, project = model)
#> Compiling
#>   [Note] This may take a minute.
#>   [Note] Use 'showCompilerOutput = TRUE' to see C++ compilation details.

# Run the sampler

samples <- runMCMC(
  Cmcmc,
  niter = 20000,
  nburnin = 5000,
  thin = 10,
  nchains = 4,
  samplesAsCodaMCMC = TRUE
)
#> running chain 1...
#>   [Note] NUTS sampler (nodes: beta_tr[1, 1], beta_tr[1, 2], sigma0, sigma1, mu0, mu1) is using 5000 warmup iterations.
#>          Since `warmupMode` is 'default' and `nburnin` > 0,
#>          the number of warmup iterations is equal to `nburnin`.
#>          The burnin samples will be discarded, and all samples returned will be post-warmup.
#> |-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
#> |-------------------------------------------------------|
#> running chain 2...
#> |-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
#> |-------------------------------------------------------|
#> running chain 3...
#> |-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
#> |-------------------------------------------------------|
#> running chain 4...
#> |-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
#> |-------------------------------------------------------|

# Diagnosis

library(coda)

#plot(samples)
summary(samples)
#> 
#> Iterations = 1:1500
#> Thinning interval = 1 
#> Number of chains = 4 
#> Sample size per chain = 1500 
#> 
#> 1. Empirical mean and standard deviation for each variable,
#>    plus standard error of the mean:
#> 
#>                  Mean     SD Naive SE Time-series SE
#> beta[1, 1]    -1.1515 0.2339 0.003020       0.003021
#> beta[1, 2]     0.9434 0.3067 0.003959       0.003987
#> beta_tr[1, 1] -0.1611 0.8614 0.011120       0.011095
#> beta_tr[1, 2]  0.1262 0.8775 0.011329       0.011109
#> mu0           -0.8638 1.2632 0.016308       0.016159
#> mu1            0.7187 1.2798 0.016522       0.016353
#> sigma0         1.4000 0.8620 0.011128       0.011222
#> sigma1         1.3839 0.8604 0.011108       0.010829
#> 
#> 2. Quantiles for each variable:
#> 
#>                   2.5%      25%     50%     75%   97.5%
#> beta[1, 1]    -1.62178 -1.30778 -1.1422 -0.9906 -0.7023
#> beta[1, 2]     0.35792  0.73219  0.9402  1.1411  1.5611
#> beta_tr[1, 1] -1.84650 -0.72992 -0.1688  0.4168  1.5433
#> beta_tr[1, 2] -1.65034 -0.45582  0.1533  0.6985  1.8113
#> mu0           -3.30615 -1.55660 -0.9832 -0.2464  2.0065
#> mu1           -2.09567  0.06155  0.7878  1.4156  3.2337
#> sigma0         0.05980  0.64194  1.3551  2.1279  2.9165
#> sigma1         0.05682  0.62358  1.3407  2.1066  2.9087
gelman.diag(samples)
#> Potential scale reduction factors:
#> 
#>               Point est. Upper C.I.
#> beta[1, 1]         1.000       1.00
#> beta[1, 2]         1.000       1.00
#> beta_tr[1, 1]      1.000       1.00
#> beta_tr[1, 2]      1.001       1.00
#> mu0                1.001       1.00
#> mu1                1.002       1.01
#> sigma0             0.999       1.00
#> sigma1             1.000       1.00
#> 
#> Multivariate psrf
#> 
#> 1
effectiveSize(samples)
#>    beta[1, 1]    beta[1, 2] beta_tr[1, 1] beta_tr[1, 2]           mu0 
#>      6000.000      5925.066      6029.638      6264.186      6147.237 
#>           mu1        sigma0        sigma1 
#>      6141.437      5905.630      6362.195

all_samples <- do.call(rbind, samples)

beta_nimble2 <- all_samples[, "beta[1, 1]"]

Plot and compare

Code 
df <- data.frame(
  value = c(beta_stan, beta_jags, beta_nimble1, beta_nimble2),
  source = factor(rep(
    c("Stan", "JAGS", "NIMBLE", "NIMBLE - HMC"),
    times = c(length(beta_stan), length(beta_jags), length(beta_nimble1), length(beta_nimble2))
  ))
)

library(ggplot2)

ggplot(df, aes(x = value, color = source)) +
  geom_density(size = 1) +
  labs(title = "Posterior density of beta[1,1]",
       x = "beta[1,1]",
       y = "Density") +
  theme_minimal()
#> Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
#> ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.
Source Code
---
title: "Logistic BHM - RStan v JAGS v Nimble - 1"
output:
  rmarkdown::html_vignette:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 2
    number_sections: true
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Two-arm trial TFP v RStan}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>"
)

# Check if running on GitHub Actions
# This is important as otherwise Github will attempt to build this and it's missing the python libraries and will fail
#on_github <- Sys.getenv("GITHUB_ACTIONS") == "true"

# If on GitHub, set eval = FALSE for all subsequent chunks
#knitr::opts_chunk$set(eval = !on_github)

```

### Quickstart
This vignette compares the same Bayesian hierarchical model fitted first with Rstan and then with Vegas. The model is a logistic regression. Only key snippets and outputs are shown below. The full vignette is available [here](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/GSK-Biostatistics/vignettes-asia/refs/heads/main/vegas/stanvegas1_jax.qmd). For the equivalent vignette but which uses Apple's MLX framework to run the computation's on a GPU see [here](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/GSK-Biostatistics/vignettes-asia/refs/heads/main/vegas/stanvegas1_mlx.qmd). If available the latter approach is preferred as considerably computationally faster.

### Model Formulation

The model implemented here is a non-centered parameterization (helps improve MCMC sampling) for a logistic model.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_0 &\sim \text{Normal}(0, 2.5)\\
\sigma_0 &\sim \text{Half-Normal}(0, 2.5) \\
\mu_1 &\sim \text{Normal}(0, 2.5) \\
\sigma_1 &\sim \text{Half-Normal}(0, 2.5) \\
\tilde{\theta}_j &\sim \text{Normal}(0, 1)\quad\enspace\text{for}\enspace{j=1,2}  \\
\beta_0 &= \mu_0 + \sigma_0 \tilde{\theta}_1 \\
\beta_1 &= \mu_1 + \sigma_1 \tilde{\theta}_2 \\
\text{logit(}{p_i}) &= \beta_0 + \beta_1 z_i\quad\quad\enspace\enspace\text{for}\enspace{i=1,\dots,N}  \\
y_i &\sim \text{Bernoulli}(p_i)
\end{aligned}
$$


```{r rsetup, include=FALSE,eval=TRUE}
library(rstan)
library(rjags)
library(coda)
library(nimble)
library(nimbleHMC)
library(nimbleSCR)
library(bayesplot)
library(ggplot2)
library(kableExtra)
```

## Data set

The R chunk below creates a simple dataset of three cols:

-   a binary response variable y (0/1 = non-responder/responder),
-   a (dummy) basket ID variable (=1)
-   a binary treatment variable (0/1 = control/test treatment)

The basket ID is currently set fixed at 1, denoting there is only one basket in this trial, i.e. a classical two arm randomized trial design. Baskets will be added in other vignettes.

```{r datasets, include=TRUE,eval=TRUE}
### Prepare model inputs
set.seed(99999)
# Set up data
rr_k_ctrl <- c(0.20)        # control response rate for each basket
rr_k_trt <- c(0.40)         # treatment response rate for each basket

K<-length(rr_k_ctrl)        # number of baskets

N_k_ctrl <- rep(100, K)     # number of control participants per basket
N_k_trt <- rep(100, K)      # number of treatment participants per basket
N_k <- N_k_ctrl + N_k_trt   # number of participants per basket (both arms combined)
N <- sum(N_k)               # total sample size
k_vec <- rep(1:K, N_k)      # N x 1 vector of basket indicators (1 to K)

z_vec<-NULL;
y<-NULL;
for(i in 1:K){ # for each basket repeat 0-control 1-trt according to the specifc Ns
  z_vec<-c(z_vec,rep(0:1,c(N_k_ctrl[i],N_k_trt[i]))) # treatment/control indicator
  y<-c(y,
       c(rbinom(N_k_ctrl[i],1,rr_k_ctrl[i]), # bernoulli for control
         rbinom(N_k_trt[i],1,rr_k_trt[i]))) #           for trt
}

thedata<-data.frame(y,basketID=k_vec,Treatment=z_vec)

```


```{r datasetsQ, include=TRUE,eval=TRUE}
my_table1_object <- head(thedata) %>%
  kable() %>%
  kable_styling(full_width = F) %>%
  column_spec(1, bold = F)#, color = "blue")

my_table1_object

```

## RStan

The code for implementing the above model is RStan is given below. 

```{r stanmodeldef,include=TRUE,echo=TRUE,eval=TRUE}
### Stan model
  BHM_stan_1 <- "data {
  int<lower = 0> K;                     // number of baskets (K >= 2)
  int<lower = 0> N;                     // total number of participants
  //int<lower = 0, upper = N> N_k[K];     // K x 1 vector of basket sample sizes
  int<lower = 0, upper = N> N_k;
  int<lower = 1, upper = K> k_vec[N];   // N x 1 vector of basket indicators
  int<lower = 0, upper = 1> z_vec[N];   // N x 1 vector of treatment indicators for active treatment arm
  int<lower = 0, upper = 1> y[N];       // N x 1 vector of binary responses
}
parameters {
  matrix[K,2] beta_tr;                  // K x 2 matrix of transformed regression coefficients
  real mu1;                              // scalar of hierarchical mean (log odds ratio)
  real<lower = 0> sigma1;                  // scalar of hierarchical SD (log odds ratio)
  real mu0;                              // scalar of hierarchical mean (log odds ratio)
  real<lower = 0> sigma0;                  // scalar of hierarchical SD (log odds ratio)

}
transformed parameters {
  matrix[K,2] beta;                     // K x 2 matrix of regression coefficients (without transformation)
  // Calculate beta coefs using transformed betas (leads to better mixing)
  for (k in 1:K){
    beta[k,1] = mu0 + sigma0 * beta_tr[k,1];
    beta[k,2] = mu1 + sigma1 * beta_tr[k,2];
  }

}
model {
  mu0 ~ normal(0, 2.5);    // vectorized normal priors for hierarchical means (specify SD in normal distn)
  sigma0 ~ normal(0, 2.5);       // vectorized half-normal priors for hierarchical SDs (specify SD in normal distn)
  mu1 ~ normal(0, 2.5);    // vectorized normal priors for hierarchical means (specify SD in normal distn)
  sigma1 ~ normal(0, 2.5);       // vectorized half-normal priors for hierarchical SDs (specify SD in normal distn)
  beta_tr[,1] ~ normal(0, 1);  // vectorized normal prior for the log odds for the control arm
  beta_tr[,2] ~ normal(0, 1);  // vectorized normal prior for the log odds ratios
  for (i in 1:N)
    y[i] ~ bernoulli_logit(beta[k_vec[i], 1] + beta[k_vec[i], 2] * z_vec[i]);
}
"

```



```{r stansample,echo=TRUE,include=FALSE,results='hide',eval=TRUE}

# Create list with input values for Stan model
data_input_1 <- list(
  K = K,                # number of subgroups
  N = N,                # total sample size
  N_k = N_k,            # sample size per basket
  k_vec = k_vec,        # N x 1 vector of subgroup indicators
  z_vec = z_vec,        # N x 1 vector of active treatment arm indicators
  y = y                 # N x 1 vector of binary responses
  )



### Compile and fit model
options(mc.cores = parallel::detectCores())
# Compile model (only run the line below once for a simulation study - compilation not dependent on any
# simulation inputs as defined by scenarios or on simulated data)
stan_mod_1 <- stan_model(model_code = BHM_stan_1)
# Fit model
nsamps <- 30000       # number of posterior samples (after removing burn-in) per chain
nburnin <- 1000       # number of burn-in samples to remove at beginning of each chain
nchains <- 4          # number of chains
BHM_pars_1 <- c("mu0", "sigma0","mu1", "sigma1", "beta","beta_tr")     # parameters to sample
start_time <- Sys.time()
stan_fit_2 <- sampling(stan_mod_1, data = data_input_1, pars = BHM_pars_1,
                       iter = nsamps + nburnin, warmup = nburnin, chains = nchains)
end_time <- Sys.time()
end_time - start_time
post_draws_2 <- as.matrix(stan_fit_2)           # posterior samples of each parameter

beta_stan <- extract(stan_fit_2,"beta[1,1]")$"beta[1,1]"

```

### Diagnostics
```{r}
# Log posterior
lp_fit_2 <- log_posterior(stan_fit_2)
head(lp_fit_2)

#Nuts patterns
np_fit_2 <- nuts_params(stan_fit_2)
head(np_fit_2)

#mcmc parcoord
color_scheme_set("darkgray")
posterior_fit_2 <- as.array(stan_fit_2)
mcmc_parcoord(posterior_fit_2, np = np_fit_2)



```

### Summarize results

```{r stanresults,fig.width=10, fig.height=6, out.width="100%",eval=TRUE}
library(bayesplot)
color_scheme_set("viridisA")
p<-mcmc_trace(stan_fit_2,"mu0")
#ggsave("precomputed/vg2_traceplot1_stan.png", p)
print(p)

plot(stan_fit_2)
```


### JAGS

```{r}
BHM_jags_2 <- "
model {
for (i in 1:N) {
    p[i] <- ilogit(beta[k_vec[i], 1] + beta[k_vec[i], 2] * z_vec[i])
    y[i] ~ dbern(p[i])
  }

  for (k in 1:K) {
    beta_tr[k, 1] ~ dnorm(0, 1)
    beta_tr[k, 2] ~ dnorm(0, 1)
  }
  
  for (j in 1:K){
    beta[j,1] <- mu0 + sigma0 * beta_tr[j,1]
    beta[j,2] <- mu1 + sigma1 * beta_tr[j,2]
  }

  #tau0 <- 1 / (sigma0^2)
  #tau1 <- 1 / (sigma1^2)

  tau_mu <- 1 / (2.5^2)

  mu0 ~ dnorm(0, tau_mu)
  mu1 ~ dnorm(0, tau_mu)

  sigma0 ~ dunif(0, 3)
  sigma1 ~ dunif(0, 3)
}
"

bhm_jags_mod <- jags.model(
  textConnection(BHM_jags_2),
  data = data_input_1,
  n.chains = 4,
  n.adapt = 10000
)

update(bhm_jags_mod, 10000)

jags_mod_sim = coda.samples(model = bhm_jags_mod,
                       variable.names = c("mu0", "sigma0","mu1", "sigma1", "beta","beta_tr"),
                       n.iter = 10000, thin =1)

summary(jags_mod_sim)

#plot(jags_mod_sim)

mat_jags <- as.matrix(jags_mod_sim)
beta_jags <- mat_jags[, "beta[1,1]"]
```


### NIMBLE with default sampler

```{r}
nimble_code_1 <- nimbleCode({  

  for (i in 1:N) {
    p[i] <- ilogit(beta[k_vec[i], 1] + beta[k_vec[i], 2] * z_vec[i])
    y[i] ~ dbern(p[i])
  }

  for (k in 1:K) {
    beta_tr[k, 1] ~ dnorm(0, 1)
    beta_tr[k, 2] ~ dnorm(0, 1)
  }
  
  for (j in 1:K){
    beta[j,1] <- mu0 + sigma0 * beta_tr[j,1]
    beta[j,2] <- mu1 + sigma1 * beta_tr[j,2]
  }

  #tau0 <- 1 / (sigma0^2)
  #tau1 <- 1 / (sigma1^2)

  tau_mu <- 1 / (2.5^2)

  mu0 ~ dnorm(0, tau_mu)
  mu1 ~ dnorm(0, tau_mu)

  sigma0 ~ dunif(0, 3)
  sigma1 ~ dunif(0, 3)
  }

) 

#Initial values

K_val <- data_input_1$K
N_val <- data_input_1$N

inits <- list(
  mu0 = 0,
  mu1 = 0,
  sigma0 = 1,
  sigma1 = 1,
  beta_tr = matrix(0, nrow = K_val, ncol = 2),
  beta = matrix(0, nrow = K_val, ncol = 2)
)

# Build model


model <- nimbleModel(
  nimble_code_1,
  # Ensure k_vec is in constants, not data
  constants = list(
    N = N_val,
    K = K_val,
    k_vec = data_input_1$k_vec,
    z_vec = data_input_1$z_vec
  ),
  data = list(
    y = data_input_1$y
  ),
  inits = inits,
  dimensions = list(
    beta_tr = c(K_val, 2),
    beta = c(K_val, 2),
    k_vec = c(N_val)    
  )
)

#Configure the default sampler
# NIMBLE automatically detects conjugacy (Gibbs) and assigns 
# Adaptive Metropolis to non-conjugate parameters.
mcmcConf <- configureMCMC(model)
mcmcConf$addMonitors("beta")
#conf$addMonitors(c("mu0", "mu1", "sigma0", "sigma1", "beta_tr", "beta"))



#Build MCMC
mcmc <- buildMCMC(mcmcConf)

# Compile
Cmodel <- compileNimble(model)

# Build and compile HMC sampler
Cmcmc <- compileNimble(mcmc, project = model)

# Run the sampler

samples <- runMCMC(
  Cmcmc,
  niter = 20000,
  nburnin = 5000,
  thin = 10,
  nchains = 4,
  samplesAsCodaMCMC = TRUE
)

# Diagnosis

library(coda)

#plot(samples)
summary(samples)
gelman.diag(samples)
effectiveSize(samples)

all_samples <- do.call(rbind, samples)

beta_nimble1 <- all_samples[, "beta[1, 1]"]

```


### NIMBLE with HMC

```{r}
nimble_code_1 <- nimbleCode({  

  for (i in 1:N) {
    p[i] <- ilogit(beta[k_vec[i], 1] + beta[k_vec[i], 2] * z_vec[i])
    y[i] ~ dbern(p[i])
  }

  for (k in 1:K) {
    beta_tr[k, 1] ~ dnorm(0, 1)
    beta_tr[k, 2] ~ dnorm(0, 1)
  }
  
  for (j in 1:K){
    beta[j,1] <- mu0 + sigma0 * beta_tr[j,1]
    beta[j,2] <- mu1 + sigma1 * beta_tr[j,2]
  }

  #tau0 <- 1 / (sigma0^2)
  #tau1 <- 1 / (sigma1^2)

  tau_mu <- 1 / (2.5^2)

  mu0 ~ dnorm(0, tau_mu)
  mu1 ~ dnorm(0, tau_mu)

  sigma0 ~ dunif(0, 3)
  sigma1 ~ dunif(0, 3)
  }

) 

#Initial values

K_val <- data_input_1$K
N_val <- data_input_1$N

inits <- list(
  mu0 = 0,
  mu1 = 0,
  sigma0 = 1,
  sigma1 = 1,
  beta_tr = matrix(0, nrow = K_val, ncol = 2),
  beta = matrix(0, nrow = K_val, ncol = 2)
)

# Build model


model <- nimbleModel(
  nimble_code_1,
  # Ensure k_vec is in constants, not data
  constants = list(
    N = N_val,
    K = K_val,
    k_vec = data_input_1$k_vec,
    z_vec = data_input_1$z_vec
  ),
  data = list(
    y = data_input_1$y
  ),
  inits = inits,
  dimensions = list(
    beta_tr = c(K_val, 2),
    beta = c(K_val, 2),
    k_vec = c(N_val)    # This is often the missing piece!
  ),
  buildDerivs = TRUE
)

# Compile
Cmodel <- compileNimble(model)

# Key step: Configure HMC

conf <- configureHMC(model)
conf$addMonitors(c("mu0", "mu1", "sigma0", "sigma1", "beta_tr", "beta"))
conf$printSamplers()

# Build and compile HMC sampler
Rmcmc <- buildMCMC(conf)
Cmcmc <- compileNimble(Rmcmc, project = model)

# Run the sampler

samples <- runMCMC(
  Cmcmc,
  niter = 20000,
  nburnin = 5000,
  thin = 10,
  nchains = 4,
  samplesAsCodaMCMC = TRUE
)

# Diagnosis

library(coda)

#plot(samples)
summary(samples)
gelman.diag(samples)
effectiveSize(samples)

all_samples <- do.call(rbind, samples)

beta_nimble2 <- all_samples[, "beta[1, 1]"]

```

### Plot and compare

```{r, plot_res}
df <- data.frame(
  value = c(beta_stan, beta_jags, beta_nimble1, beta_nimble2),
  source = factor(rep(
    c("Stan", "JAGS", "NIMBLE", "NIMBLE - HMC"),
    times = c(length(beta_stan), length(beta_jags), length(beta_nimble1), length(beta_nimble2))
  ))
)

library(ggplot2)

ggplot(df, aes(x = value, color = source)) +
  geom_density(size = 1) +
  labs(title = "Posterior density of beta[1,1]",
       x = "beta[1,1]",
       y = "Density") +
  theme_minimal()
```
